speaker: interviewer
text: --conducting an interview with Manya Winshell. Going back, Manya, to when your baby was in the hospital in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tashkent. Which is in the country of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uzbekistan.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the baby was very sick. Were you allowed to visit the baby? How-- how did they make those arrangements?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once or twice a week, they would let me visit. But I was sitting there outside all the time, day and night. So the nurse that he had there was a Jewish woman from Kiev, but she wasn't nice. Then one time the same night, there was a Christian woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the beginning of his stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Towards--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a long time already in the hospital. And he didn't eat, he didn't nothing. He was unconscious.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Christian woman nurse felt sorry for me. She thought he's going to die. So she figured she let me in. So she let me in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had grapes with me. So I was putting-- on a-- she gave me a teaspoon-- the grape juice in his mouth, and he was swallowing, swallowing. And then when it got light-- it was night-- he opened his eyes and recognized me. That's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [SOBBING] can't express what I felt. [CRYING] And at that morning, we took our baby. We signed papers. And we went away. We took out the baby. And we took him [INAUDIBLE] to Kolkhoz, Uzbekistan. They-- they sent us there. We couldn't go just by ourselves. And there, it's Muslims. But they were very good to us. And that's where I saved my baby. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stayed there eight months in that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you have shelter?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an unfinished building that they let us stay there. We stayed. No beds. No-- I was dreaming of having a bed one day. I stayed with the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your food?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, while we were in Kolkhoz, we were getting bread. There was 400 gram to a person. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would that be equivalent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a pound, bread. So we had bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be for how long? That-- that amount of bread would be for--

speaker: interviewee
text: For the whole day. That's all. But we found some things to eat. There was trees, like I don't know how to call it. It was like raspberries. But the Muslims, the people there, wouldn't eat it. They say, if you eat that, you die. But we tasted it. And I knew it's OK. So we were eating all. My husband would go up in the tree and shake it and fall-- fell down [INAUDIBLE] on the ground. And we had food for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about hygiene?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. No baths. No nothing. Hygiene.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: You get water. You washed yourself a little. That's all. Nothing. You couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothes?

speaker: interviewer
text: --the latrine?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the field. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went out in the fields. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your baby, at this time, was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He get well.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was about a year and a half already when we left there. And we left there, and we went-- because my husband's brother was in Kazakhstan. That's a different country. Soviet Union, but Kazakhstan. So we went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. It took us maybe a month to get there, because freight trains. Couldn't get anything else. You couldn't buy a ticket and just go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get the ticket?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. They let us on a train, took. We went all the way to Kyzylorda. That's Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we were talking a little while ago, you said that you were with other Jews in the park. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, lot of Jews. Hundreds. Hundreds. Thousands.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Jews help Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Nobody helped anybody at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very hard times. Everybody was looking out to get something to eat. That's all. It was very hard. But when we got to Kyzylorda, to Kazakhstan-- the city's Kyzylorda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that for me, please? Try.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kyzyl-- Kyzylorda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know the meaning of it? That's red ord.

speaker: interviewer
text: Red?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, the meaning of that in--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what's 'ord,' the [INAUDIBLE] used to have ords, like [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a bunch, you know, of people. That's-- that's what they called it. Kyzylorda. And, anyway, in Kazakhstan. Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it's-- Kazakhstan is all the time in the news, because they got the most nuclear weapon-- weapons in the world now, Kazakhstan. So that's where I spent a lot of time in that place, in Kyzylorda in Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kazakhstan. And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Kazakhstan, again, is in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: It is in. Kazakhstan is in-- country? Where is it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kazakhstan is a country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Country.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Soviet Union at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it's a country for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's in the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not now. Now, it's Kazakhstan. And we stayed there until 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till 1946.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever in a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were never in a ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I escaped before they put me in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My parents were, all the family, but not me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have news?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing. We didn't know anything what's going on there. Because in Russia, you can't just turn on a television or a radio the way you want it, where you want it. They have, like, speakers, and there's one station and everybody hears the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Kazakhstan, what was life like there for you and your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there was already OK, because my husband worked. And he got some money, and we got bread. Everybody got 400 gram of bread. And the money he made, we could buy potatoes or whatever we could. No luxuries. No meat or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your child at this age was?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was already two years, two and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you said you stayed there till 1946.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then he was older.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What-- when your-- what did you tell your child? What did you-- how did you explain what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't know any better, since he was born, you know. When he was a year old, I escaped with him. How would he know any other life? That's what he knew. So it was nothing to explain to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about religious holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: What religious holidays? Nobody even knew what's-- when it's the holiday. How would we know? The Soviet Union, there was no religion. And I didn't know what day it was, that the holidays or anything, until we came back to Europe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you are in Russia. It's 1946.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946, We went back to Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They let us. From Russia, they let us go to Poland. We said, we're going to Poland. And Poland was, at that time, communist. So they let us go back. But when we came to Poland, there were pogroms. They were killing Jews after the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we tried to get into-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were boys from Israel. It was called the You can write it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Just go back for a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that they were killing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were killing Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was killing the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polacks were killing the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were making pogroms that we couldn't stay there. So we went to-- the guys from Israel helped us to get to Czechoslovakia. And when we got to Czechoslovakia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they let-- they-- the Red Cross came already there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was a place where everybody came, like a transit place. And from there, they took us with train-- with the train to DP camps in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from Czechoslovakia, you went on trains--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: In between, this was a transit camp that you were in in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A transit camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not a DP camp, but just a transit. Anybody stopped there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just going back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for a minute, you said you went back to Poland. And things were very bad there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We couldn't stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the pogroms. Did you go to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't. I knew already everything, what was happening. And I wouldn't go. I was even afraid to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you informed of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because some people went, and they told us. I knew that nobody's alive, because I didn't tell you that I was writing to my town, to all the friends, Christian friends, to tell me--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? When were you writing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944. Because it was already free from the Germans. So '44, '45, I was writing all the time and nobody answered. But somehow, a Jewish girl was working already in the post office. And she got my letters. She knew who I was. And she knew about my sister. I told you my youngest sister escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is-- the-- her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. So she wrote me a letter with the address of my sister. And that's how my sister came to Kazakhstan. She was near Moscow. And it wasn't easy. They wouldn't let her go, because you-- you needed special-- special [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know how to say it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Like, you get a visa to go from one place to another.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, she couldn't come to me. And finally, she did. And when she came, that was a terrible thing. She came in, and I didn't-- I didn't know she's coming. And she knocked on the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we had already a place to-- to live. And she walked in. My husband opened the door. She walked in. And she's standing there, and I didn't recognize her. Because when she escaped she was a young kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she when you last saw her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was about 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's '45. It's five years later, four and a half years later. And I didn't recognize her. And I see the woman standing and crying. So finally, I realized it's my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What thoughts went through your head at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [SOBBING] That was a terrible thing. I couldn't recognize my own sister. [CRYING] First of all, she looked like a, you know, from the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a girl from-- you know, the Russian girls with the babushkas with the-- I couldn't recognize her. You can imagine we-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What words were said between you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah. Who can remember? [CRYING] We were both crying maybe two days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And since then, she stayed with me all the time. [CRYING] She has a nice story to tell. She escaped, a young kid, alone. Ah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister stayed with you for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she went to DP camp with me. We were together. She got married in a DP camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Linz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are now in what country? From Czechoslovakia, you went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw your sister for the first time in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Kazakhstan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went with you to the DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She went together with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who else was with you? Who else was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, my baby. That's all. And she.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you're in a DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year is?

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had nothing to do with the Austrians. They took us to the DP camp. The Red Cross took us to DP camp. And there was all Jewish people in the DP camp. And nobody could get in. We could get out from DP camp. But nobody can come in. We had our own police.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were permitted out. You were permitted to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But we couldn't go to work any place.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had to return at the end of the day? You had to be back there at the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. If-- [LAUGHS] where else would we go? Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you would have been permitted if you had a place to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions in the DP camp? Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had to live on what the United Jewish Appeal gave, what the Red Cross. That's all. Nobody could go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Red Cross, what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some things, food. That's all. And if there was-- somebody got sick, we had a doctor in the-- in the DP camp and they would take us to hospital in the town, in the city, wherever it was, not in the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first DP camp that you went to in Austria, where-- what city in Austria did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Linz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Linz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And from there, we went to Braunau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in the DP camp for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the DP-- in Linz, we were really a short time. And then we came to Braunau and from--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a time were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Braunau? A few months. And then we went back to Linz. There was a DP camp in Linz. We stayed until '4-- '49, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to understand, you were in first DP camp in Austria was Braunau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed there for approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was still there with you, your-- your-- who?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, the baby, and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Braunau, you went to another DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. At Linz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Linz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is in--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Ebelsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ebelsberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1949. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there, in the '46-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was already '47 until '49, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in a DP camp? What was life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: But you weren't afraid of they're going to kill us. We-- I had my family. You know, we had to clean, work. We had a room, a big room, one room, the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ebelsberg, who was with you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, my child, and I had my daughter already. She was born in DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your daughter was-- your daughter was born in Ebelsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Braunau.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Braunau. In what year was she born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you had two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now, I had two children. I wanted very much another child. She's named after my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about education in these camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a school that the camp made, the camp, the people in the camp. And one of the people was a teacher. Didn't have to be a real teacher. Didn't need license. But they taught the children a little Hebrew, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about general studies? Did the child learn about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My son was eight years old already when he came to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they put him right away into the second grade. He didn't know the language, didn't know anything. And he made High School of Science. If you are from New York, you know High School of Science.

speaker: interviewer
text: Getting back, though, to the second camp in Ebelsberg. You were there until what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Until '49. So you were there altogether how many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: From '49, eight. It was '46, '40-- almost three years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they wouldn't let us into the United States. We had to wait. And I had all the papers. I had a big family in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, going back again, I wanted to know at any time in your experience going--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from Russia on to all the places that you did go, did you ever have reason to hide? Did you ever hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not there. Only when I escaped from Tuczyn. We were hiding. The Ukrainians shouldn't know that we're running away. So we were hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you-- where did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: In woods on the way until we got into the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Ukrainians treat the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. [LAUGHS] You know, during-- I wasn't already there when they were killing. They didn't need Nazis there. The Ukrainians did it. They killed the people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was killed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by a Ukrainian that he knew all his life with a hatchet.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: People told me later. Some people survived. He did it in the woods. And another guy was in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hiding, and he saw it. That Ukrainian killed my father and his son, the guy that told me that. Then I met him in a DP camp, and he told me everything-- from my town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Winshell--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you ever find the necessity for concealing your identity at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Even when I went to in the Russian Tambov, I say the people were wonderful to us. They didn't know I'm Jewish. They thought we are Polacks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never told them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the people in Tambov think of Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never asked them. You know, they are anti-Semitic. I didn't ask anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hide your-- conceal your identity? How did you do it? In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way? They ask us from where we come. We are Polacks. We are from Poland. We escaped. That's all. And they were wonderful to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not knowing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. You had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the most painful time for you in all of those experiences, the most painful?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my child was in the hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they all told me he's not going to survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all I had from home, my baby. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Couldn't be worse. But I was very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you are getting ready to go to the United States. You waited how many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years in DP camp until they let me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you pick the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I had here a whole family, my mother's brother, my mother's sister, my husband's mother's brother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of cousins. That's why I wanted to come here. And I couldn't go to Israel anyway. Because you couldn't get in legally. And I couldn't go with my baby. My daughter was a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your daughter was how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to the United States?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. My sister was married already. She came by herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister married at--

speaker: interviewee
text: In DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your expectations in coming to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be free. To have freedom. Isn't that the right way?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What do you mean how? By boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think 10 days. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that name? And that was the name of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The boat. And I came the 23 of March, 1949. I remember dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what that was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- it was a terrible voyage. Because it was an army boat. And we were all seasick. My husband was sick the whole-- whole time until we came to the United States.
